Defendant Jonathan Geffard'S Demurrer To Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-23-2014 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Plaintiff LEONARD BAZA, Tel No. (415) 746-9730 appearing in pro per;
Atty. G. DANIEL NEWLAND of Seyfarth Shaw, LLP, Tel No. (415) 397-2823 appearing for defendants The Hillstone Restaurant Group, Inc. and Jonathan Geffard.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT JONATHAN GEFFARD'S Demurrer To Complaint is sustained. While the receipt of services by Plaintiff from Defendant does not deprive Plaintiff of standing, the Court can and does find as a matter of law that Defendant's dress code is not arbitrary but rather is facially valid because it bears a reasonable relationship to commercial objectives appropriate to an enterprise serving the public. (Harris v. Capital Growth Investors XIV (1991) 52 Cal.3d 1142, 1165.) Plaintiff's intentional infliction of emotional distress claim fails because the Court can determine as a matter of law that Defendant's alleged conduct could not be determined by a trier of fact to be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1050-51.) As Defendant prudently notes, "restaurants ask patrons to comply with dress codes, and even provide subpar service, every single day." As to Plaintiff's negligent infliction of emotional distress claim, while there is no duty to avoid negligently causing emotional distress, as a matter of law a defendant can be liable for this tort if the emotional distress arises out of a defendant's breach of some other legal duty and the emotional distress is proximately caused by that breach of duty. (Potter v. Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. (1993) 6 Cal.4th 965, 984.) Here, Plaintiff alleges that Defendant had a statutory duty pursuant to the Unruh Act, and that Defendant breached that duty. However, Plaintiff cannot establish as a matter of law that Defendant breached that duty by enforcing its dress code. As the defects in the Complaint cannot be cured by amendment, the Demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Ann Delgado, CSR #11185 (302/EHG)

Defendant The Hillstone Restaurant Group, Inc.'S Demurrer To Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-23-2014 Granted Per Written Order

APPEARANCES: Plaintiff LEONARD BAZA, Tel No. (415) 746-9730 appearing in pro per;
Atty. G. DANIEL NEWLAND of Seyfarth Shaw, LLP, Tel No. (415) 397-2823 appearing for defendants The Hillstone Restaurant Group, Inc. and Jonathan Geffard.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT THE HILLSTONE RESTAURANT GROUP, INC.'S Demurrer To Complaint is sustained. While the receipt of services by Plaintiff from Defendant does not deprive Plaintiff of standing, the Court can and does find as a matter of law that Defendant's dress code is not arbitrary but rather is facially valid because it bears a reasonable relationship to commercial objectives appropriate to an enterprise serving the public. (Harris v. Capital Growth Investors XIV (1991) 52 Cal.3d 1142, 1165.) Plaintiff's intentional infliction of emotional distress claim fails because the Court can determine as a matter of law that Defendant's alleged conduct could not be determined by a trier of fact to be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1050-51.) As Defendant prudently notes, "restaurants ask patrons to comply with dress codes, and even provide subpar service, every single day." As to Plaintiff's negligent infliction of emotional distress claim, while there is no duty to avoid negligently causing emotional distress, as a matter of law a defendant can be liable for this tort if the emotional distress arises out of a defendant's breach of some other legal duty and the emotional distress is proximately caused by that breach of duty. (Potter v. Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. (1993) 6 Cal.4th 965, 984.) Here, Plaintiff alleges that Defendant had a statutory duty pursuant to the Unruh Act, and that Defendant breached that duty. However, Plaintiff cannot establish as a matter of law that Defendant breached that duty by enforcing its dress code. As the defects in the Complaint cannot be cured by amendment, the Demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H.
